title: Fixed points and crossovers for the hysteresis scaling of dynamic mean-field models
abstract: Phase transitions are divided into first-order phase transitions and continuous ones in current classification. While the latter shows striking phenomena of scaling and universality, the former is generically characterized by discontinuous jumps in extensive variables and pronounced hysteresis. Recent studies have demonstrated universal scaling behavior controlled by a cubic fixed point in first-order phase transitions. However, more recent investigations into the hysteresis in a dynamic mean-field quartic model driven through its first-order phase transitions have revealed new scaling exponents for different driving rates. Here, we discover a new exponent for large driving rates arising surprisingly from critical phenomena and show that, depending on the magnitude of the driving rates and on the absence or presence of noise, the same mean-field model remarkably exhibits several universality classes with definite universal scaling exponents governed by their corresponding fixed points through a systematic scaling analysis based on renormalization group theory. The theories and their various crossovers between different fixed points along with complete universal scaling of full curve collapse are verified by numerical results. This further confirms universal scaling in first-order phase transitions.
summary: This paper identifies new universal scaling exponents for hysteresis in first-order phase transitions within a mean-field framework. It shows that different physical regimes, controlled by driving rates and noise, correspond to distinct universality classes. The study provides a unified scaling theory that explains previously observed non-universal behaviors as crossovers between fixed points.
main_problem: The study investigates the existence of universal scaling behavior and crossovers between different fixed points in the hysteresis of first-order phase transitions.
main_result: The authors discover a new scaling exponent of 3/5 for large driving rates in a quartic model and demonstrate that a single model can exhibit multiple universality classes (quadratic, cubic, and quartic) depending on driving rate and noise.
method: The researchers use Renormalization Group (RG) theory for scaling analysis, Finite-Time Scaling (FTS) to treat the driving rate as a finite timescale, and numerical simulations to verify curve collapse.
model_or_system: A dynamic mean-field quartic (phi^4) model governed by a Langevin equation with Gaussian white noise, driven by a linearly varying external field.
key_observables: Magnetization (M), coercivity (Hc), and the external field (H).
important_parameters: Driving rate (R), noise amplitude (sigma), reduced temperature (a2), and coupling constants (a4).
main_assumptions: The analysis is restricted to mean-field models and assumes the absence of anomalous dimensions, which allows for full curve collapse.
figures_summary: Figures show the dependence of coercivity on driving rate, various rescaling plots (quadratic, cubic, quartic) demonstrating data collapse, and infinite-order rescaling showing a unified magnetization curve.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of hysteresis scaling, defines the dynamic mean-field model, applies RG theory to identify fixed points and exponents, presents numerical verification of crossovers and curve collapse, and discusses the impact of noise and spatial extensions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Coercivity Hc versus R at ﬁxed a4 = 100 with varying a2. (b) Hc versus R at ﬁxed a2 = −100 with varying a4. Both (a) and (b) correspond to σ = 0. Dashed lines with numerical labels in all panels indicate the theoretical (crossover) slopes from Eqs. (19)–(24) and (26). (c) and (d) Quadratic rescaling of (a) and (b) by Eq. (11). (e) and (f) Cubic rescaling (a) and (b) by Eqs. (12) and (24). (g) and (h) Quartic rescaling of (a) and (b) by Eq. (13). (i) Inﬁnite-order rescaling of (b) by Eq. (14). (j) and (k) H0 −Hs at Ms versus R for identical parameter sets as (a) and (b). (l) and (m) Cubic scaling of (j) and (k) by Eq. (15). (n) and (o) Hc versus R for the same parameters as (a)...

FIG. 2. (a) Magnetization curves for a series of R values, as listed in the legend of (b). The curves shift to the right with increasing R. a2 = a20(R/R0)−1/2, a4 = a40(R/R0)−1/2, and σ = σ0(R/R0)−1/2, a20 = −400, a40 = 948, σ0 = 0.4, R0 = 10. (b) Rescaled curves from (a) based on the inﬁnite- order behavior, Eq. (14) .
